context_update.summary: This chunk discusses limitations of criterion [10] and model assumptions. It notes that the derivation of [10] makes it only semiquantitative, that the percolation model [4] is inaccurate at high liquid fractions, that the constant n in [10] is hard to measure, and that treating defects as ellipsoidal permeability regions is oversimplified. Despite these, the authors conclude that small dendritic defects greatly assist channel segregation and that criterion [10] is more reliable than [1] for real castings. A key finding is that channel segregates can occur even when interdendritic flow is substantially parallel to isotherms, because out-of-plane defects can redirect flow toward segregation. No experimental data, material systems, or processing routes are specified; the discussion remains theoretical. Future chunks may address specific alloys, validation, or extensions.

It notes that the derivation of [10] makes it only semiquantitative, that the percolation model [4] is inaccurate at high liquid fractions, that the constant n in [10] is hard to measure, and that treating defects as ellipsoidal permeability regions is oversimplified. Despite these, the authors conclude that small dendritic defects greatly assist channel segregation and that criterion [10] is more reliable than [1] for real castings. A key finding is that channel segregates can occur even when interdendritic flow is substantially parallel to isotherms, because out-of-plane defects can redirect flow toward segregation. No experimental data, material systems, or processing routes are specified; the discussion remains theoretical. Future chunks may address specific alloys, validation, or extensions.
Key variables: liquid fraction; constant n; defect orientation relative to isotherms; permeability; criterion [1]; criterion [10]; percolation model [4]
Microstructure: dendrite network defects
Mechanisms: defect-assisted channel segregation growth; flow diversion by out-of-plane defects
